payload.text:
crie uma calculadora de custos de impressão 3d
ela deverá conter:
Nome da Peça
Tempo (horas)
Peso (gramas)
Custo Filamento (R$/kg)
Potência Impressora (W)
Custo kWh (R$)
Consumo (kWh)
Valor Rolo Embalagem (R$)
Embalagens por Rolo
Unidades/Mês 
Custo Fixo Mensal (R$) 
Valor da Impressora (R$)
Vida Útil (horas)
% de Falhas 
Imposto (%)
Taxa Cartão (%)
Custo Anúncio (%)
Markup (multiplicador)

Faça com base nas [Imagens Anexadas] Faça Parecido Mas Não Igual

**Initiating Design Concepts**

I'm focusing now on the React app's core design. I'm prioritizing cost calculation logic, starting with filament, energy, and depreciation. I'm considering modular components for each cost type, allowing easy updates and expansion. The interface will likely feature image input and cost breakdown displays.


**Designing the Interface**

I've got the overall interface design mapped out. It aims for a similar look to the provided images but with my own spin, using Tailwind CSS. I'm deep into analyzing the requirements now, breaking them down into inputs and outputs, and planning the data flow. The input fields are forming up, including the power and cost/kWh, which I'll use to calculate the energy consumption. I'm focusing on those crucial calculations now.


**Analyzing Profitability Metrics**

I'm now fully immersed in the profitability metrics. I'm focusing on calculating the final selling price, gross profit, and net profit. I'm also planning detailed breakdowns for taxes and fees. The tech stack has also been confirmed: React 18+ with TypeScript, Tailwind CSS for styling, Lucide React for icons, and potentially Recharts for visualization (though I might keep it simpler). `jspdf` will handle the "Export PDF" functionality. I'm keeping a clean, card-based design in mind.


**Structuring the React App**

I'm currently focused on the React app's structure and component design. I'm prioritizing the implementation of reusable components. I've broken them down into input groups, input fields with labels and tooltips, a summary card, and a pricing card for displaying the final price and profit calculations. For PDF exports, I'm leaning toward using html2canvas and jspdf to capture the state and generate a printable receipt. I'm also refining the core mathematical logic for accurate cost calculations.


**Defining Profit Calculations**

I'm now clarifying the profit calculations. I've realized the markup covers the profit, and taxes/fees are deducted from the final price to determine net profit. I'm focusing on accurately translating those calculations into code, aiming for a clean and efficient structure. This requires defining the inputs, icons, and layout for the app.


**Finalizing App Structure**

I've got the React app's core components now solidified. I'm focusing on `CalculatorState` as the central data model. I've designed `InputField`, `Section` components, and moved the calculation logic into `calculations.ts` for clean code. The `App.tsx` file now orchestrates the main layout, state, and integration.


**Implementing Print Functionality**

I've decided to leverage the browser's native print functionality for "exporting" the final results. This is the simplest and most effective way to ensure a clean, print-friendly output without introducing unnecessary dependencies. I'm focusing on ensuring the UI renders well for printing, removing any specific PDF generation logic.


**Structuring the Calculator Component**

I'm now fully immersed in the design of the core `Calculator` component. I'm ensuring all imports are explicit to reduce potential conflicts. I'm double-checking the proper implementation of `lucide-react` icons. I'm focusing on preventing the infinite loop trap by carefully managing dependency arrays within hooks. I'm also addressing some UI design fixes, such as ensuring that input fields accept decimal values using the `step="0.01"` attribute. I've also added currency formatting, creating a `formatCurrency` helper function using `Intl.NumberFormat('pt-BR', ...)` for proper display. Now, I'm replicating the structure from the reference image, grouping the input fields into logical sections: Part Data, Material & Energy, Packaging & Fixed, Depreciation, and Taxes & Markup. Finally, I will forgo the XML output, and instead focus on creating a print layout for the final results, which will be triggered by `window.print()`.


**Calculating All the Values**

I'm deep into making all the final calculations now. I'm focusing on accurately coding the formula for `totalBaseCost` and `sellingPrice`, considering all inputs. I've successfully calculated all the core values, starting from the filament up to the final selling price. I've ensured all values flow to the right place and are based on the correct input values, and I'm adding tooltips now. I will test it with a few sets of input values.


**Implementing Final Calculations**

I'm now focusing on calculating the `taxValue`, `cardValue`, `adValue`, `totalDeductions`, and `netProfit`. I've double-checked all formulas, ensuring the values are correctly derived from the `sellingPrice` and relevant percentages. I'm focusing now on testing the entire flow with different input values to ensure accuracy.

payload.text: quando apagar o texto ao inves de virar 0 faca virar um espaco em branco

**Reviewing Input Handling**

I'm currently focused on the input field behavior within "3D Cost Calc Pro." Specifically, I'm examining how deletions are handled and the default values or forced number inputs triggered by `handleInputChange` in `App.tsx`. I believe the issue lies in the implementation details of how this function interacts with input field changes.


**Adjusting State Handling**

My focus has shifted to the `handleInputChange` function and how it influences the input field's behavior. Instead of defaulting numeric fields to '0' when cleared, I'm now exploring state modifications and input logic alterations. The goal is to allow an empty string representation for numeric fields in `formData`. I'm comparing the implications of changing the state type to accommodate empty strings vs. modifying the display value directly within `InputField`. I am leaning toward adapting the display logic while maintaining numeric state.


**Refining Data Handling**

I'm now contemplating how to effectively handle empty inputs while ensuring correct calculations within the "3D Cost Calc Pro." I'm carefully considering how data should be typed within `CostData`, and whether string fields are a viable option for numeric fields, or a separate state variable should be added. I'm leaning toward maintaining a strict numeric type in `formData` but allowing the display value to be an empty string, preventing unexpected results. I'm exploring the implications of separate state variables for display versus storage.


**Optimizing Data Typing**

I'm now revising my approach to handle empty numeric inputs, moving towards a more standard React form pattern. Instead of using separate states for display and calculation, I'm modifying `types.ts` to allow `number | ''` for the numeric fields. This ensures consistency. The plan now includes an update to `handleInputChange` in `App.tsx` where empty strings will be explicitly set and calculations will handle these as zero within a `useMemo` hook. This should fulfill the user's requirement for a blank display while preserving numeric functionality.


**Updating Data Structures**

I'm now focusing on `types.ts`, specifically the `CostData` properties. My plan involves updating these fields to accept `number | string`, to enable empty string representation. Next, I'll update `App.tsx` by setting the initial state of `formData` which can remain numbers and updating `handleInputChange` to handle empty strings correctly.


**Improving Input Validation**

My current focus is improving the input validation to allow for commas, as used in Brazilian Portuguese, within numeric input fields. The goal is to accurately parse these values and handle the resulting calculations correctly. Instead of using `parseFloat`, I am now considering a utility function, and I'm updating the `val` helper function within the calculation section to address this specific requirement.


**Enhancing Data Parsing**

I'm now revising my approach to handle empty numeric inputs, moving towards a more standard React form pattern. Instead of using separate states for display and calculation, I'm modifying `types.ts` to allow `number | string` for the numeric fields. This ensures consistency. The plan now includes an update to `handleInputChange` in `App.tsx` where empty strings will be explicitly set and calculations will handle these as zero within a `useMemo` hook. This should fulfill the user's requirement for a blank display while preserving numeric functionality. I'm focusing on ensuring values passed to the `InputField` component are strings, but the state data remains typed as numbers.



**Implementing String State**

I'm now implementing the `handleInputChange` function to handle empty strings as a valid state value. The new function includes logic to store the raw input string directly in the state, even if empty or partially typed. The update uses a conditional to check if the input is empty and if so, sets the corresponding field in the state to an empty string. If not empty, the input string is stored in state. This should preserve the user input "5." or "" as desired.


**Adjusting Value Parsing**

I'm now revising my approach to handle empty numeric inputs. I'm focusing on adapting the `getValue` function within the `results` `useMemo` hook to robustly handle numeric input as either a number or a string. Empty inputs will stay as a string, partial inputs like "5." will remain as strings. The plan also includes a modification of the `handleInputChange` function to ensure that the input value, whether a number or empty string, is passed as the state. This will maintain the user's intended display value.


**Adapting Input Parsing**

My current focus is on correctly handling user input, especially empty and partially filled numeric fields, such as "5.". I've updated the type definition in `types.ts` to allow `number | string` for all numeric fields. I'm now implementing the `handleInputChange` function to handle empty strings as valid state values, preserving the raw input string. I'm now modifying the `getValue` function to robustly handle numeric input as either a number or a string.


**Implementing Calculations Safely**

I'm currently focused on implementing robust and safe number parsing to ensure accurate calculations. I'm introducing a helper function, `parseNumber`, to handle string or number inputs consistently. The `handleInputChange` function is also being updated to directly store the input string in the state, simplifying the logic. This means I'm modifying how values are destructured in the `useMemo` hook, ensuring it always uses `parseNumber`. The plan is to ensure that empty strings are correctly handled as zero in calculations. I'm also addressing the `InputField` component's `step` and `min` attributes and making sure the input value is correctly handled, which will enable the user to input data like "5.", or empty strings directly.


**Improving Number Handling**

I'm now refining the number parsing logic, considering input variations such as commas as decimal separators. I'm adding a robust `parseNumber` function to handle potential comma characters that may appear. The current state is already compatible with `number | string`. Now I'm updating the `handleInputChange` function to directly store the input string, simplifying the logic in the calculation. I'm also ensuring the user input "5." or "" is preserved. This will be ensured by the safe number parsing in the `useMemo` calculation.

To implement the requested change (allowing input fields to be blank instead of defaulting to 0 when cleared), I need to update the state management to handle strings (specifically empty strings) for numeric fields, and then parse them back to numbers only during calculation.

Here are the updates:

1.  **types.ts**: Update the `CostData` interface to allow properties to be `number | string`.
2.  **App.tsx**:
    *   Update `handleInputChange` to directly store the input value (string) in the state, allowing empty strings.
    *   Create a helper function `getNumber` to safely convert the state values (which might be empty strings) into numbers for the mathematical calculations.

entries.description: Update CostData interface to allow string values for numeric fields to support empty inputs.
Update handleInputChange to allow empty strings and use helper function to parse numbers for calculations.

payload.text: Aplique um modo escuro a toda a aplicação, incluindo header, footer, e seções de cards. Certifique-se de que as cores sejam contrastantes e legíveis.

**Exploring Dark Mode Implementation**

I'm currently investigating how to implement a dark mode for the entire application, which mainly entails dealing with the existing Tailwind CDN setup. I need to figure out the best approach: whether to enable dark mode directly within Tailwind or manually apply dark classes. The user's request for dark mode to be "applied" suggests it needs to be either the default, toggleable, or just... I'm leaning toward a toggle.


**Clarifying User Intent**

I'm now carefully considering whether the user wants a persistent dark mode, a toggle, or to simply adapt existing styles. The "Apply dark mode" prompt could imply a system-level change, a toggle, or just a thematic update. I'm leaning toward implementing a toggle for flexibility, which will involve modifying the `index.html` file.


**Analyzing Files and Logic**

I'm now diving into the `index.html`, `App.tsx`, `InputField.tsx`, and `SectionCard.tsx` files. I need to add the `dark:` prefixes to relevant classes for dark mode. For `index.html`, I'll either add `class="dark"` or implement logic for a toggle on the `<html>` tag and update the `body`'s background color. In `InputField.tsx`, I'm looking at dark backgrounds for inputs and light text. The `SectionCard.tsx` also needs similar adjustments.


**Finalizing Implementation Steps**

I'm solidifying my approach: modifying `index.html` to configure Tailwind for class-based dark mode and initialize the theme from storage/system preferences. In `App.tsx`, I'll introduce a state for `isDarkMode`, add a toggle button, and apply `dark:` classes throughout. Input and SectionCard adjustments are also planned, with consideration for existing colors. The "Resultado Final" card's indigo coloration requires inspection for contrast.


**Confirming Style Adjustments**

I've finalized the dark mode styles for SectionCard.tsx and InputField.tsx, focusing on contrast and readability. These include changes to backgrounds, text colors, and borders, ensuring visual clarity in dark mode. Next, I'll update the tailwind config and the index.html file to work together.


**Integrating Theme Toggle**

I'm now incorporating a dark mode toggle into `App.tsx`. I'll introduce state to manage the dark/light theme, and also the implementation of the button itself. This will update the class on the `<html>` element, which is already configured for Tailwind dark mode, making the changes reflect across the application. I'm also considering the best practices for storing and retrieving the selected theme state persistently.


**Applying Specific Style Changes**

I'm making a fine-grained dive into each section. I've pinpointed that the text in various stat fields needs `dark:text-slate-200` and `dark:text-slate-400`. The "Composição de Custos" card will now also have the specified dark mode classes. The "Resultado Final" card seems okay, I'll need to check the shadow. I'm thinking of adjusting the `index.html` file to properly apply these, and then I'll look into some finer points.


**Implementing Dynamic Dark Mode**

I'm now integrating dynamic dark mode classes within `SectionCard.tsx`. To ensure proper handling by the Tailwind CDN, I'm carefully constructing the class strings. I'll need to check the exact behavior when color variables are used with template literals, especially with the CDN setup. While the initial setup seemed functional, I'm taking precautions to prevent future conflicts, by checking a few scenarios or refactoring as needed. I'll test the behavior in different cases, making sure to avoid potential problems.


**Finalizing Toggle Integration**

I'm now integrating the theme toggle and applying its logic throughout.  I've added the `Sun` and `Moon` icons, implemented the toggle function using a boolean state in `App`, and updated the main layout elements.  I also applied the dark mode classes to the "Composição" and "Tips" cards, and the footer to match the design.  The main `index.html` file has the `darkMode: 'class'` configuration and fallback background styles. I'm satisfied and will now move on to testing the integration and accessibility.


**Implementing Card Style Updates**

I'm now revising the `SectionCard.tsx` styles.  I'm changing the existing classes, particularly the background, border, and shadow classes, to include dark mode variations.  Specifically, I'm updating `bg-white`, `border-slate-100`, and the shadow class with their respective dark mode counterparts like `dark:bg-slate-800` and `dark:border-slate-700`.  I'm focusing on the code updates now.

To apply a comprehensive dark mode with high contrast and legibility, I will update the Tailwind configuration to use the 'class' strategy and modify the components to support dark color tokens.

Here are the changes:

1.  **`index.html`**: Configure Tailwind for `darkMode: 'class'` so we can manually toggle it.
2.  **`App.tsx`**: Add a toggle button (Sun/Moon), manage the theme state, and apply dark mode classes to the layout, headers, footers, and specific cards like the Cost Composition and Pricing/Profit cards.
3.  **`components/SectionCard.tsx`**: Update the card container and dynamic icon colors to be legible in dark mode (using darker backgrounds and lighter text for the icons).
4.  **`components/InputField.tsx`**: Update input backgrounds, borders, and text colors to be dark-grey with light text, ensuring good contrast against the dark card backgrounds.

entries.description: Enable Tailwind dark mode 'class' strategy and set dark background for body.
Add dark mode classes for inputs, labels, and tooltips.
Add dark mode support for card containers, headers, and dynamic icon colors.
Implement dark mode toggle logic, update layout classes for dark mode, style the specific calculation result cards for dark mode, and update text colors throughout.
